cwe: CWE-327 - Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
CWE-330 - Use of Insufficiently Random Values
CWE-338 - Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
CWE-1240 - Use of a Cryptographic Primitive with a Risky Implementation

threat.title: Weak Cryptographic Key Generation and Insufficient Random Number Generation
threat.description: When applications use weak random number generators (RNGs) or improper key generation techniques for cryptographic keys, SSH keys, TLS certificates, or encryption keys, attackers can predict or brute force the keys to decrypt sensitive data, impersonate servers, or compromise encrypted communications. The infamous Debian OpenSSL vulnerability (CVE-2008-0166) reduced key entropy from 2^128 to ~32,000 possible keys, affecting millions of SSH keys and SSL certificates for over 2 years. Weak key generation has led to Bitcoin wallet thefts, SSH key collisions, and mass cryptographic failures.

versions_affected: All platforms with weak RNG implementations
technology.ecosystem: OpenSSL, Java SecureRandom, Python secrets, Go crypto/rand, Node.js crypto

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies application using weak RNG for cryptographic operations (e.g., seeded with timestamp or process ID)
2. Collects multiple public keys, session tokens, or encrypted data samples from the target system
3. Analyzes the samples to identify patterns or predictability in key generation
4. Uses specialized tools (e.g., PRNG crackers, factorization attacks) to recover private keys or predict future keys
5. In Debian OpenSSL case: Downloads list of ~32,000 possible SSH keys generated by vulnerable OpenSSL version
6. Attempts authentication to SSH servers using the compromised key database
7. Successfully gains unauthorized access to servers, decrypts TLS traffic, or compromises cryptographic operations
8. For Bitcoin wallets: Generates all possible weak private keys and checks blockchain for associated funds
prerequisites: Application uses weak PRNG (e.g., math.random, time-based seeds, insufficient entropy sources)
Cryptographic keys generated with less than 128 bits of entropy
No Hardware Security Module (HSM) or secure key generation service
Keys generated in predictable environments (containers with low entropy)
Lack of entropy monitoring or key strength validation

cve_references.description: Debian OpenSSL PRNG vulnerability: OpenSSL in Debian was patched incorrectly, removing entropy sources and reducing key space from 2^128 to ~32,000 possible keys. Affected millions of SSH keys, SSL certificates, and cryptographic keys generated between 2006-2008
Python random module used for Django password reset tokens: Django used Python's random.random() for generating password reset tokens, allowing attackers to predict tokens and take over accounts
Apache Struts 2 weak random number generation for session IDs and CSRF tokens allowing session hijacking
Apache Tomcat CGIServlet on Windows uses weak RNG for session ID generation allowing session prediction
Go language encoding/pem weak random IV generation in PEM encryption allowing plaintext recovery

real_world_breaches.incident: Debian OpenSSL Weak Key Generation Crisis
real_world_breaches.impact: Millions of SSH keys, SSL/TLS certificates, OpenVPN keys, and DNSSEC keys generated over 2 years were predictable. Affected virtually every Debian/Ubuntu system. Required mass key regeneration and certificate revocation worldwide
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Debian maintainer removed two lines from OpenSSL PRNG initialization to fix a Valgrind warning, inadvertently removing all entropy sources except process ID. Only ~32,768 possible keys for RSA/DSA

real_world_breaches.impact: Multiple Bitcoin wallets compromised due to weak random number generation. Attackers generated all possible weak private keys and checked blockchain for associated funds. Estimated losses: thousands of BTC
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Bitcoin wallet implementations on Android and embedded systems used weak entropy sources (e.g., /dev/urandom without proper initialization, time-based seeds)

real_world_breaches.impact: Researchers scanned Internet-wide SSH servers and found thousands of duplicate keys due to weak RNG in embedded devices. Many routers, firewalls, and IoT devices shared identical SSH host keys
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Embedded devices generated SSH keys during first boot with insufficient entropy (/dev/random not seeded), leading to identical keys across devices of same model

real_world_breaches.impact: 184 Taiwanese government-issued digital certificates found to have weak RSA keys (512-bit) that could be factored in hours using cloud computing
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Smart card manufacturer used inadequate key length and weak random number generation in certificate generation process

real_world_breaches.impact: Cryptocat secure messaging app used Math.random() for DSA key generation, allowing attackers to recover private keys and decrypt messages
real_world_breaches.root_cause: JavaScript crypto implementation used Math.random() instead of window.crypto.getRandomValues() for cryptographic operations

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Weak RSA key generation in payment API allowing private key recovery through factorization attack. 1024-bit keys generated with weak prime selection
IoT device SSH keys predictable due to timestamp-based seeding. All devices with same manufacturing batch shared subset of 256 possible keys

security_research.title: Mining Your Ps and Qs: Detection of Widespread Weak Keys in Network Devices
security_research.key_finding: Internet-wide scan revealed 5.57% of TLS hosts and 9.60% of SSH hosts share keys due to weak random number generation. Found repeated RSA moduli allowing private key recovery through GCD computation

security_research.title: Ron was wrong, Whit is right
security_research.key_finding: Analyzed 6.2 million RSA public keys from web servers. Found 12,934 distinct moduli sharing prime factors with other keys, allowing private key extraction via factorization

security_research.title: The Million-Key Question: Investigating the Origins of RSA Public Keys
security_research.key_finding: Classified RSA keys by implementation through statistical analysis. Found smartcards and TPMs with weak key generation patterns allowing key clustering and potential compromise

security_research.title: NIST SP 800-90A: Recommendation for Random Number Generation Using Deterministic Random Bit Generators
security_research.key_finding: Provides requirements for cryptographically secure random number generators. Emphasizes minimum 128 bits of entropy for cryptographic keys and proper seeding from hardware entropy sources

mitigations.title: Use crypto/rand in Go for Secure Key Generation
mitigations.description: Go's crypto/rand package provides cryptographically secure random number generation using the operating system's CSPRNG. For RSA key generation, use crypto/rsa.GenerateKey with minimum 2048-bit keys (preferably 4096-bit for long-term security).
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
validation_steps: Verify RSA keys are minimum 2048 bits (preferably 4096 bits)
Ensure crypto/rand.Reader is used, never math/rand
Check /proc/sys/kernel/random/entropy_avail shows >128 bits before key generation
Validate generated keys with privateKey.Validate()
Test key generation succeeds and produces unique keys on each run
Verify private key file permissions are 0600

mitigations.title: Java SecureRandom with Strong Algorithms
mitigations.description: Use java.security.SecureRandom with strong algorithms (NativePRNGNonBlocking, DRBG) for key generation. Avoid java.util.Random for any cryptographic operations. Configure JCA providers to use /dev/urandom on Unix systems.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
validation_steps: Verify SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong() or NativePRNGNonBlocking is used
Check RSA keys are minimum 2048 bits (4096 recommended)
For AES, use 256-bit keys for maximum security
Verify private key files have 0600 permissions
Test on Linux: cat /proc/sys/kernel/random/entropy_avail shows >128 bits
Never use java.util.Random for cryptographic operations

mitigations.title: Python secrets Module and cryptography Library
mitigations.description: Use Python's secrets module (3.6+) for token generation and cryptography library for key generation. Avoid random module for cryptographic purposes. Use os.urandom() as fallback for Python < 3.6.

code_example.code:
import secrets
import os
import hashlib
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa, ec
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization, hashes
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.ciphers import Cipher, algorithms, modes
import base64

# VULNERABLE - DO NOT USE
def generate_weak_key():
    """DO NOT USE - This is insecure!"""
    import random
    random.seed()  # Time-based seed is predictable
    return random.randbytes(32)  # INSECURE!

# SECURE: Generate RSA key pair
def generate_rsa_keypair(key_size=4096):
    """
    Generate RSA key pair with strong randomness.
    Minimum 2048 bits, recommended 4096 bits.
    """
    if key_size < 2048:
        raise ValueError(f"RSA key size must be at least 2048 bits, got {key_size}")
    
    # Generate private key
    private_key = rsa.generate_private_key(
        public_exponent=65537,
        key_size=key_size,
        backend=default_backend()
    )
    
    # Extract public key
    public_key = private_key.public_key()
    
    return private_key, public_key

# Save RSA private key to file
def save_private_key(private_key, filename, password=None):
    """
    Save private key to PEM file with optional encryption.
    """
    if password:
        encryption = serialization.BestAvailableEncryption(password.encode())
    else:
        encryption = serialization.NoEncryption()
    
    pem = private_key.private_bytes(
        encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        format=serialization.PrivateFormat.PKCS8,
        encryption_algorithm=encryption
    )
    
    # Write with restricted permissions (0600)
    with open(filename, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(pem)
    os.chmod(filename, 0o600)

# Save RSA public key to file
def save_public_key(public_key, filename):
    """
    Save public key to PEM file.
    """
    pem = public_key.public_bytes(
        encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        format=serialization.PublicFormat.SubjectPublicKeyInfo
    )
    
    with open(filename, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(pem)

# Generate ECDSA key pair
def generate_ecdsa_keypair(curve_name='SECP256R1'):
    """
    Generate ECDSA key pair using specified curve.
    Supported: SECP256R1, SECP384R1, SECP521R1
    """
    curves = {
        'SECP256R1': ec.SECP256R1(),
        'SECP384R1': ec.SECP384R1(),
        'SECP521R1': ec.SECP521R1(),
    }
    
    if curve_name not in curves:
        raise ValueError(f"Unsupported curve: {curve_name}")
    
    private_key = ec.generate_private_key(
        curves[curve_name],
        backend=default_backend()
    )
    
    public_key = private_key.public_key()
    return private_key, public_key

# Generate AES-256 key
def generate_aes_key(key_size=256):
    """
    Generate AES encryption key.
    Supported sizes: 128, 192, 256 bits
    """
    if key_size not in [128, 192, 256]:
        raise ValueError("AES key size must be 128, 192, or 256 bits")
    
    return secrets.token_bytes(key_size // 8)

# Generate secure random bytes
def generate_random_bytes(n):
    """
    Generate n cryptographically secure random bytes.
    """
    return secrets.token_bytes(n)

# Generate URL-safe token
def generate_secure_token(nbytes=32):
    """
    Generate URL-safe base64-encoded token.
    """
    return secrets.token_urlsafe(nbytes)

# Generate hex token
def generate_hex_token(nbytes=32):
    """
    Generate hex-encoded token.
    """
    return secrets.token_hex(nbytes)

# Generate numeric OTP
def generate_otp(length=6):
    """
    Generate cryptographically secure numeric OTP.
    """
    return ''.join(str(secrets.randbelow(10)) for _ in range(length))

# Generate random password
def generate_password(length=20):
    """
    Generate strong random password.
    """
    import string
    alphabet = string.ascii_letters + string.digits + string.punctuation
    return ''.join(secrets.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length))

# Check entropy availability (Linux/Unix)
def check_entropy_available():
    """
    Check available entropy on Linux/Unix systems.
    """
    try:
        with open('/proc/sys/kernel/random/entropy_avail', 'r') as f:
            entropy = int(f.read().strip())
            return entropy
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return None  # Not available on this platform

# Test random number quality
def test_randomness(samples=10000):
    """
    Basic randomness quality test.
    """
    data = secrets.token_bytes(samples)
    
    # Count zeros (should be ~1/256 of total)
    zero_count = data.count(0)
    zero_percentage = (zero_count / samples) * 100
    
    # Calculate byte distribution uniformity
    byte_counts = [data.count(i) for i in range(256)]
    avg_count = sum(byte_counts) / 256
    variance = sum((c - avg_count) ** 2 for c in byte_counts) / 256
    
    print(f"Random quality test (n={samples}):")
    print(f"  Zero bytes: {zero_count} ({zero_percentage:.2f}%, expected ~0.39%)")
    print(f"  Distribution variance: {variance:.2f}")
    print(f"  Result: {'PASS' if variance < avg_count * 2 else 'FAIL'}")

def main():
    print("=== Secure Key Generation Examples ===")
    
    # Check system entropy
    entropy = check_entropy_available()
    if entropy is not None:
        print(f"\nSystem entropy: {entropy} bits")
        if entropy < 128:
            print("WARNING: Low entropy. Key generation may block.")
    
    # Test randomness quality
    print("\n")
    test_randomness(10000)
    
    # Generate RSA key pair
    print("\nGenerating RSA 4096-bit key pair...")
    private_key, public_key = generate_rsa_keypair(4096)
    save_private_key(private_key, 'rsa_private.pem', password='strongpassword')
    save_public_key(public_key, 'rsa_public.pem')
    print("RSA key pair generated and saved")
    
    # Generate ECDSA key pair
    print("\nGenerating ECDSA key pair (SECP256R1)...")
    ec_private, ec_public = generate_ecdsa_keypair('SECP256R1')
    print("ECDSA key pair generated")
    
    # Generate AES-256 key
    print("\nGenerating AES-256 key...")
    aes_key = generate_aes_key(256)
    print(f"AES-256 Key (hex): {aes_key.hex()}")
    
    # Generate secure tokens
    print("\nGenerating secure tokens...")
    print(f"URL-safe token: {generate_secure_token(32)}")
    print(f"Hex token: {generate_hex_token(32)}")
    print(f"Numeric OTP: {generate_otp(6)}")
    print(f"Random password: {generate_password(20)}")
    
    # Generate random bytes
    print("\nGenerating 32 random bytes...")
    random_bytes = generate_random_bytes(32)
    print(f"Random bytes (hex): {random_bytes.hex()}")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
validation_steps: Use secrets module for all token generation
Use cryptography library for RSA/ECDSA key generation
Verify RSA keys are minimum 2048 bits (4096 recommended)
Check private key files have 0600 permissions
Test: python -c 'import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))'
Never use random module for security-sensitive operations

mitigations.title: Hardware Security Modules (HSM) for Production Key Management
mitigations.description: For production environments handling sensitive data, use Hardware Security Modules (HSM) or managed key management services (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, GCP KMS) to generate and store cryptographic keys. HSMs provide FIPS 140-2 Level 2/3 validated random number generation and tamper-resistant key storage.

code_example.framework: AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault / GCP KMS
code_example.code:
# AWS KMS Example
import boto3
import base64
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError

class AWSKMSKeyManager:
    """
    AWS KMS integration for secure key generation and management.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, region_name='us-east-1'):
        self.kms_client = boto3.client('kms', region_name=region_name)
    
    def create_customer_master_key(self, description, key_spec='SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT'):
        """
        Create a Customer Master Key (CMK) in AWS KMS.
        Key specs: SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT, RSA_2048, RSA_4096, ECC_NIST_P256, etc.
        """
        try:
            response = self.kms_client.create_key(
                Description=description,
                KeyUsage='ENCRYPT_DECRYPT',
                KeySpec=key_spec,
                Origin='AWS_KMS',  # KMS generates key material using FIPS 140-2 Level 2 HSM
                MultiRegion=False,
                Tags=[
                    {'TagKey': 'Environment', 'TagValue': 'Production'},
                    {'TagKey': 'Purpose', 'TagValue': 'DataEncryption'}
                ]
            )
            
            key_id = response['KeyMetadata']['KeyId']
            print(f"Created CMK: {key_id}")
            return key_id
            
        except ClientError as e:
            print(f"Error creating key: {e}")
            raise
    
    def generate_data_key(self, key_id, key_spec='AES_256'):
        """
        Generate a data encryption key (DEK) for envelope encryption.
        Returns plaintext key and encrypted key.
        """
        try:
            response = self.kms_client.generate_data_key(
                KeyId=key_id,
                KeySpec=key_spec
            )
            
            return {
                'plaintext_key': response['Plaintext'],
                'encrypted_key': response['CiphertextBlob']
            }
            
        except ClientError as e:
            print(f"Error generating data key: {e}")
            raise
    
    def generate_random_bytes(self, num_bytes=32):
        """
        Generate cryptographically secure random bytes using KMS HSM.
        """
        try:
            response = self.kms_client.generate_random(NumberOfBytes=num_bytes)
            return response['Plaintext']
        except ClientError as e:
            print(f"Error generating random bytes: {e}")
            raise

# Azure Key Vault Example
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.keyvault.keys import KeyClient
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

class AzureKeyVaultManager:
    """
    Azure Key Vault integration for secure key management.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, vault_url):
        credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
        self.key_client = KeyClient(vault_url=vault_url, credential=credential)
        self.secret_client = SecretClient(vault_url=vault_url, credential=credential)
    
    def create_rsa_key(self, key_name, key_size=4096):
        """
        Create RSA key in Azure Key Vault HSM.
        Key size: 2048, 3072, or 4096
        """
        try:
            # Create key in HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated)
            key = self.key_client.create_rsa_key(
                name=key_name,
                size=key_size,
                hardware_protected=True,  # Store in HSM
                key_operations=['encrypt', 'decrypt', 'sign', 'verify']
            )
            
            print(f"Created RSA key: {key.name}")
            return key
            
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error creating key: {e}")
            raise
    
    def create_ec_key(self, key_name, curve='P-256'):
        """
        Create Elliptic Curve key in Azure Key Vault.
        Curves: P-256, P-384, P-521, P-256K (secp256k1)
        """
        try:
            key = self.key_client.create_ec_key(
                name=key_name,
                curve=curve,
                hardware_protected=True
            )
            
            print(f"Created EC key: {key.name}")
            return key
            
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error creating EC key: {e}")
            raise

# Google Cloud KMS Example
from google.cloud import kms
import os

class GCPKMSManager:
    """
    Google Cloud KMS integration for key management.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, project_id, location_id='us-east1'):
        self.client = kms.KeyManagementServiceClient()
        self.project_id = project_id
        self.location_id = location_id
    
    def create_key_ring(self, key_ring_id):
        """
        Create a key ring to hold cryptographic keys.
        """
        location_name = f"projects/{self.project_id}/locations/{self.location_id}"
        key_ring = {}
        
        try:
            request = {
                'parent': location_name,
                'key_ring_id': key_ring_id,
                'key_ring': key_ring
            }
            
            response = self.client.create_key_ring(request=request)
            print(f"Created key ring: {response.name}")
            return response
            
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error creating key ring: {e}")
            raise
    
    def create_crypto_key(self, key_ring_id, crypto_key_id, purpose='ENCRYPT_DECRYPT'):
        """
        Create a cryptographic key in Cloud KMS.
        Purpose: ENCRYPT_DECRYPT, ASYMMETRIC_SIGN, ASYMMETRIC_DECRYPT
        """
        key_ring_name = (
            f"projects/{self.project_id}/locations/{self.location_id}/"
            f"keyRings/{key_ring_id}"
        )
        
        # Use HSM protection level (FIPS 140-2 Level 3)
        crypto_key = {
            'purpose': getattr(kms.CryptoKey.CryptoKeyPurpose, purpose),
            'version_template': {
                'algorithm': kms.CryptoKeyVersion.CryptoKeyVersionAlgorithm.GOOGLE_SYMMETRIC_ENCRYPTION,
                'protection_level': kms.ProtectionLevel.HSM
            }
        }
        
        try:
            request = {
                'parent': key_ring_name,
                'crypto_key_id': crypto_key_id,
                'crypto_key': crypto_key
            }
            
            response = self.client.create_crypto_key(request=request)
            print(f"Created crypto key: {response.name}")
            return response
            
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error creating crypto key: {e}")
            raise

# OpenSSL with Hardware RNG
import subprocess

def generate_key_with_openssl_hardware_rng():
    """
    Generate RSA key using OpenSSL with hardware RNG if available.
    Checks for RDRAND (Intel) or RDSEED instructions.
    """
    # Check for hardware RNG support
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ['openssl', 'version', '-a'],
            capture_output=True,
            text=True
        )
        
        print("OpenSSL configuration:")
        print(result.stdout)
        
        # Generate RSA key with OpenSSL
        print("\nGenerating RSA 4096-bit key with OpenSSL...")
        subprocess.run(
            ['openssl', 'genrsa', '-out', 'rsa_private_openssl.pem', '4096'],
            check=True
        )
        
        # Set proper permissions
        os.chmod('rsa_private_openssl.pem', 0o600)
        print("RSA key generated successfully")
        
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e:
        print(f"Error generating key: {e}")
        raise

def main():
    print("=== HSM and KMS Key Generation Examples ===")
    
    # AWS KMS Example
    print("\n1. AWS KMS Example:")
    try:
        aws_kms = AWSKMSKeyManager()
        # Generate random bytes using HSM
        random_bytes = aws_kms.generate_random_bytes(32)
        print(f"Generated 32 random bytes from AWS KMS HSM: {base64.b64encode(random_bytes).decode()}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"AWS KMS not configured: {e}")
    
    # Azure Key Vault Example
    print("\n2. Azure Key Vault Example:")
    print("(Configure vault_url to test)")
    
    # GCP KMS Example
    print("\n3. Google Cloud KMS Example:")
    print("(Configure project_id to test)")
    
    # OpenSSL with hardware RNG
    print("\n4. OpenSSL Hardware RNG:")
    try:
        generate_key_with_openssl_hardware_rng()
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"OpenSSL error: {e}")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

pci_dss: Requirement 3.5.3 - Store cryptographic keys in HSM
validation_steps: Verify HSM/KMS integration is configured and operational
Confirm keys are generated within HSM (check protection_level or KeyOrigin)
Test key generation uses FIPS 140-2 Level 2 or higher validated modules
Implement key rotation policies (annual or bi-annual)
Monitor key usage and access patterns
Ensure key backups are encrypted and stored securely

indicators: Multiple SSH authentication failures followed by success (weak key brute force)
TLS certificate with weak RSA key (<2048 bits) or duplicate modulus
Identical cryptographic keys across multiple systems or users
Pattern recognition in generated tokens or session IDs
Successful authentication using keys from Debian weak key database
High volume of key generation operations with low entropy
Failed entropy pool warnings in system logs

log_queries.query:
index=linux_logs sourcetype=syslog
("SSH: Server listening" OR "sshd" OR "key generation")
| search "RSA" OR "DSA" OR "ECDSA"
| rex field=_raw "key fingerprint (?<fingerprint>[\w:]+)"
| stats count by fingerprint, host
| where count > 1
| eval severity="Critical"
| table fingerprint, count, host, severity
| sort -count

// Detect weak RSA key usage
Syslog
| where Facility == "authpriv" or Facility == "auth"
| where SyslogMessage contains "RSA" or SyslogMessage contains "key"
| extend KeySize = extract(@"(\d{3,4})[-\s]bit", 1, SyslogMessage)
| where toint(KeySize) < 2048
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, KeySize, SyslogMessage
| extend Severity = "High"

// Detect duplicate SSH key fingerprints
event.category: "authentication" AND system.auth.ssh.event: "Accepted"
| stats count by system.auth.ssh.fingerprint, host.name
| where count > 10
| sort count desc

fields @timestamp, @message
| filter @message like /KMS|GenerateRandom|GenerateDataKey/
| stats count() as requests by bin(5m)
| filter requests > 1000

tools.name: Factorable.net tools
tools.usage: Check for duplicate RSA moduli and factorable keys

compliance: ISO27001 (A.10.1.1 - Cryptographic Controls, A.10.1.2 - Key Management)
NIST 800-53 (SC-12 - Cryptographic Key Establishment, SC-13 - Cryptographic Protection)
